sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **catch up (with)** — reach the same point/level as
**move in** — start living in a new house, etc
**put away** — return sth to where it belongs
**wash up** — wash plates, cups, cutlery, etc

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: You must show your ...... before boarding the plane.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is iD for travel, so the correct answer is "identity card".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "identity card", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Drinking water before bed is a good ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is regular behaviour, so the correct answer is "habit".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "habit" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Drinking water before bed is a good ...... .".

items.sentence: According to my ......, the lesson starts at nine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is timetable, so the correct answer is "schedule".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "schedule" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "According to my ......, the lesson starts at nine.".

items.sentence: The boy finally had to ...... breaking the vase.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is confess, so the correct answer is "admit".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The local ...... organises activities for teenagers every Friday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is teen activities, so the correct answer is "youth club".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Jack is ...... for feeding the dog this week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is in charge of, so the correct answer is "responsible".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The whole ...... worked together after the storm.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is local people together, so the correct answer is "community".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The ...... of the village is only about 2,000 people.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is number of people, so the correct answer is "population".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: Taking things from shops without paying is
items.originalSentence: Taking things from shops without paying is **illegal / familiar**.
items.completedSentence: Taking things from shops without paying is illegal .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "illegal" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "illegal" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "familiar" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.familiar: "familiar" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The criminal appeared in **court / society** yesterday.
items.completedSentence: The criminal appeared in court yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "court" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "court" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "society" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.society: "society" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("yesterday.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: for him to forget his keys.
items.originalSentence: It's quite **typical / social** for him to forget his keys.
items.completedSentence: It's quite typical for him to forget his keys.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "typical" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "typical" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "social" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.social: "social" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("for him to forget his keys.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "vote" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "rob" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.rob: "rob" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("in elections.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: A **resident / criminal** usually lives in the area.
items.completedSentence: A resident usually lives in the area.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "resident" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "resident" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "criminal" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.criminal: "criminal" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("usually lives in the area.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: The country wants to protect its national
items.originalSentence: The country wants to protect its national **culture / routine**.
items.completedSentence: The country wants to protect its national culture .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "culture" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "culture" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "routine" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.routine: "routine" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: I usually ...... at 6:30 during the week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is start the day, so the correct answer is "get up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "get up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Please ...... early tomorrow because we have to leave at seven.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is stop sleeping, so the correct answer is "wake up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "wake up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Can you ...... your clothes after trying them on?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is tidy away, so the correct answer is "put away".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put away", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "put away" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: My brother helps ...... after dinner every evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is clean dishes, so the correct answer is "wash up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Someone tried to ...... the shop last night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is enter illegally, so the correct answer is "break into".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: After being ill, she needed time to ...... the rest of the class.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reach same level, so the correct answer is "catch up with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "catch up with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: He copied my homework, but he didn't ...... it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is avoid punishment, so the correct answer is "get away with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "get away with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Our new neighbours will ...... next weekend.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is arrive to live, so the correct answer is "move in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "move in", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
